title: Function assembly view with persistent source color matching
summary: DansAsm compiles the saved function under the cursor and pairs every mapped source row with its assembly rows using stable Godbolt-style colors plus active cursor synchronization.
details: The compile command comes from the shared compile database layer or an available C/C++/CUDA compiler fallback; the source buffer must be saved.

DWARF and CodeView file/line directives, Clang Lfunc and GCC LFB/LFE function markers, and ELF, Mach-O, and Windows labels are parsed; locations in inlined headers are visible but deliberately unpaired.
One reusable assembly buffer exists per source buffer. Results cache by command, source changedtick, function range, and optimization; bang or r bypasses the cache.
Source rows and corresponding assembly rows receive one of eight stable background/number colors. The cursor's current mapping uses a separate stronger highlight and synchronized scrolling.
n toggles compiler directives and blank noise in the generated assembly buffer, o chooses O0/O1/O2/O3/Os/Oz/Og, r recompiles, and writes auto-refresh an open view by default.
Assembly presentation never changes source bytes or source row identity.

provenance.note: Owner requested a function-scoped Godbolt-like source/assembly view.
Current parser, renderer, cache, and interaction implementation.

evidence.claim: Clang/GCC and cross-platform parsing, block selection, argument cleanup, noise display, palette stability, and inline-header unpairing are pinned.
A real compiler run opens the split, produces paired extmarks and active sync, toggles noise, and preserves source bytes/rows.

questions: How do I view and color-match one function's assembly?
Why must the source be saved before DansAsm?
How do I change optimization or hide assembly directives?

last_verified.method: Fixture parsing plus real asynchronous compile and cursor interaction.
